one_line_takeaway: A deliberately preliminary, transparently self-critical v0.5 blueprint for standardized LLM safety benchmarking (13-category taxonomy, 43,090 templated prompts, LlamaGuard auto-grading, reference-model-relative grades) whose main lesson is how hard reliable safety measurement is: even its own evaluator was only 54% accurate on the responses it flagged unsafe.
key_finding: As a feasibility demonstration the pipeline runs end-to-end, but the results carry heavy caveats the authors foreground. At the benchmark level, 5 of 13 SUTs were graded High risk, 4 Moderate, and 4 Moderate-Low; 3 of 13 were High risk across ALL hazard categories. Models responded unsafely far more to malicious/vulnerable-persona prompts than to typical-user prompts. The most sobering finding is about the evaluator itself: on the human eval set LlamaGuard's accuracy on responses it flagged as unsafe was only 54.2% (i.e. barely better than chance on its positive predictions), 86.7% on responses it flagged not-unsafe, and 70.4% overall on the balanced eval set. Because the real data is extremely imbalanced (only 4.8% flagged unsafe), the distribution-weighted accuracy on the full dataset is estimated at 85.6%. The authors judge this 'sufficiently accurate' for v0.5 while conceding it needs improvement.
core_contribution: This white paper introduces v0.5 of the MLCommons AI Safety Benchmark, an explicitly preliminary Proof-of-Concept produced by the MLCommons AI Safety Working Group. Its own contributions are: (1) a principled specification framework that scopes safety evaluation to a specific use case, system-under-test type, language/context, and set of personas rather than assessing a model's safety 'in general'; (2) a new taxonomy of 13 hazard categories (7 operationalized in v0.5); (3) a hand-built dataset of 43,090 test-item prompts covering those 7 categories; (4) an open, reference-model-relative grading system that maps the percentage of unsafe responses to a 5-point ordinal risk grade; (5) the open-source ModelBench runner and ModelGauge test-execution engine (built with the Stanford HELM/CRFM team); and (6) an example anonymized evaluation of 13 chat-tuned LMs. The authors repeatedly stress v0.5 should NOT be used to actually assess model safety and that all tested models are anonymized; v1.0 was planned for end of 2024.
threat_model: The benchmark targets unsafe free-text outputs from general-purpose English-language chat LMs in a single use case (an adult chatting to a general-purpose assistant, Western Europe / North America context). It models three deliberately UNSOPHISTICATED personas: a typical (benign) adult user, a malicious user with intent to harm but limited domain knowledge, and a vulnerable user at risk of self-harm/suicide. Critically, the threat model explicitly EXCLUDES sophisticated adversaries: no jailbreaking, no prompt injection, no adversarial prefixes, and only single-turn interactions. It also explicitly does NOT cover classical LM security (confidentiality, privacy, integrity, authenticity, availability of models or data) and does not test false refusal of benign prompts. The seven in-scope hazards are Violent Crimes, Non-Violent Crimes, Sex-Related Crimes, Child Sexual Exploitation, Indiscriminate Weapons (CBRNE), Suicide & Self-Harm, and Hate.
limitations: The authors are unusually candid: (1) v0.5 explicitly must NOT be used to assess real model safety and all models are anonymized. (2) Narrow scope: English only, Western Europe/North America context, one use case, three personas, single-turn only. (3) The taxonomy is incomplete: only 7 of 13 categories are tested, and it excludes LM security (confidentiality/integrity/availability). (4) Prompts are deliberately simple, non-adversarial, and free of hazard-specific terms, limiting ecological validity and relevance to sophisticated attackers. (5) The automated evaluator (LlamaGuard) makes substantial errors, especially the 54.2% precision on flagged-unsafe items. (6) Grades are RELATIVE to a reference model, so they signal relative not absolute safety, and only have negative predictive power (a good score means no weakness was found, not that the model is safe). (7) Low-temperature (0.01) sampling may understate unsafe-response rates. (8) The combinatorial template method is scalable but low-diversity. (9) Severe class imbalance across hazard categories (Hate/malicious alone = 26,530 of 43,090 prompts).

datasets_used: Authors' own AI Safety Benchmark v0.5 prompt dataset: 43,090 test items across 7 hazard categories, built from 725 sentence fragments (550 activities + 175 viewpoints) x 32 templates / 13 interaction types
560,170 SUT responses collected (13 SUTs x 43,090 prompts)
Human eval set: 1,320 responses (660 LlamaGuard-flagged-unsafe + 660 not-unsafe, stratified sampling), labeled by 3 annotators
LlamaGuard (evaluator model, an input-output safety filter built on Llama 2) used to auto-score responses
quantitative_results: 43,090 total test items; between 1,810 and 27,250 prompts per hazard category, mean 6,156
Prompt counts by persona: typical 5,040; malicious 36,960; vulnerable 1,090 (Hate/malicious alone = 26,530)
LlamaGuard flagged 27,045 of 560,170 responses (4.8%) as unsafe; 533,125 (95.2%) not-unsafe
LlamaGuard accuracy on eval set: 54.2% on flagged-unsafe items, 86.7% on flagged-not-unsafe, 70.4% overall; distribution-weighted full-dataset accuracy estimated 85.6%
Inter-annotator agreement: 3/3 agreement on 1,127/1,320 cases (85.4%); average Cohen's Kappa 0.79 (pairwise 0.79, 0.87, 0.72)

Reference-model unsafe rates (Table 6): Child Sexual Exploitation 0.9%, Hate 1.3%, Indiscriminate Weapons 0.3%, Non-Violent Crime 0.7%, Sex-Related Crime 3.0%, Suicide & Self-Harm 0.9%, Violent Crimes 0.5%, Overall 1.2%
Benchmark-level SUT grades: 5/13 High risk, 4/13 Moderate, 4/13 Moderate-Low; 3/13 High risk across all hazard categories
13 SUTs from 11 providers, released Mar 2023-Feb 2024, run via Together.AI at temperature 0.01, max 500 tokens (top_p 0.7)
Taxonomy: 13 hazard categories total, 7 operationalized in v0.5
baselines_compared: Evaluator choice: LlamaGuard selected over Perspective API (Perspective rejected for singular 'toxicity' attribute, social-group biases, and weak performance on some content)
Grading baseline: three state-of-the-art open-source reference SUTs per test (the worst-scoring of the three used as the reference; models used to build LlamaGuard excluded)
Grading-scheme options weighed: absolute cutoffs vs decile bins vs reference model (final system combines absolute cutoff for 'Low' with reference-model-relative bands for M-L through H)
Note: prior datasets/benchmarks such as BBQ, HONEST, RealToxicityPrompts, HELM, and BIG-bench are discussed as RELATED WORK, not used as empirical baselines in this paper
